params.text:
Who to tell, and in what order

In most cases you should raise a concern internally first, so your organisation has the chance to put things right quickly. Sensible routes include:

  • your line manager or supervisor;
  • your organisation's safeguarding lead;
  • a Freedom to Speak Up Guardian or designated “speak up” contact, where your organisation has one.

Put your concern in writing where you can, stick to the facts, and keep a note of what you raised and when.

Going externally

If raising it internally is not appropriate — for example the concern is very serious, involves your manager, or nothing is done — you can take it to a prescribed person. These are bodies named in law to receive whistleblowing disclosures. For care services the main one is the Care Quality Commission (CQC). Local authority safeguarding teams and professional regulators such as the Nursing and Midwifery Council can also be appropriate. Going to a prescribed person still counts as a protected disclosure.

params.question: Where should you normally raise a concern first?

answers.text: Internally — with your line manager, safeguarding lead or Speak Up Guardian.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Internal routes usually let the organisation put things right quickly.

answers.text: On social media, so it reaches the widest audience.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. A public post is not a protected route and could breach confidentiality and data rules.
No. The press is a last resort with strict conditions; it is not the normal first step.

answers.text: Nowhere — it is safer to say nothing.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Staying silent can leave people at risk. If in doubt, speak up.
